schools.summary: Paul introduces his correction of their error with a tender, solemn appeal: 'we beseech (entreat) you, brethren, by (hyper — concerning, and by the authority of) the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the very subject of their confusion made the ground of his appeal; 'and by our gathering together (episynagoge — the assembling, the being gathered) unto him' — the great reunion of all the saints to Christ at his coming (cf. 1 Thessalonians 4:17), the longed-for ingathering of the redeemed (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Tertullian (with Cyril): the coming and the gathering belong together — Christ's appearing and the assembling of his people to him are the one great event on which the believer's hope is fixed, the hope Paul makes the ground of his entreaty.

themes: we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ
and by our gathering together unto him
the appeal by the coming and the gathering
the great reunion of the saints to Christ

schools.summary: Paul's caution against their agitation: 'be not soon (quickly, hastily) shaken (saleuthenai — shaken as by a wave or earthquake, unsettled) in mind, or be troubled (thrown into alarm)' — they had been unsettled and frightened by a false notion; the three channels by which the error came or was claimed to come: 'by spirit (a pretended prophecy or revelation), nor by word (a spoken report or sermon), nor by letter as from us (a forged letter purporting to be Paul's)' — each falsely appealing to apostolic authority; the error itself being 'that the day of Christ is at hand (enesteken — is present, has actually arrived, is now upon us),' not merely near but already come (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: that ye be not soon shaken in mind, or be troubled
neither by spirit, nor by word, nor by letter as from us, as that the day of Christ is at hand
the warning not to be shaken
the false claim that the day had come

schools.summary: Calvin, Gill, and (with them) Henry and Barnes hold the 'man of sin... son of perdition' to be not a single individual but a kingdom or succession extending through many ages — the papal power, the Roman Antichrist, which Calvin argues from 1 John 2:18 (Antichrist already at work in many) and from the 'mystery of iniquity' working secretly then to be revealed openly. Henry: he is the 'man of sin' for his egregious wickedness and promotion of sin, the 'son of perdition' as devoted to destruction and the destroyer of many; the names and characters (v.4) 'very exactly agree' to the papal state. The 'falling away (apostasia)' is a great spiritual defection from sound doctrine, worship, and holy life, gradual but general, out of which the man of sin arises.

Cyril of Jerusalem and the fathers hold the man of sin to be a single future personal Antichrist, who comes after the falling away — the hatred and divisions among Christians making room for him — and who, as the abomination of desolation (Daniel; Matthew 24:15), sits in the temple shewing himself to be God; the apostasy precedes his personal manifestation in the last days.

A third reading (Warfield, noted by Robertson) takes the man of lawlessness as the Roman imperial line with its rage for deification (the emperor-cult), the Jewish state being the restraining power; Robertson and Ellicott stress that the figure, though described in personal terms, may be embodied in a succession (Ellicott on v.7: the prophecy is satisfied if 'he that withholdeth' is a whole succession of persons).

debates.question: Who is 'the man of sin, the son of perdition'?

sides.position: The papal power — a kingdom/succession extending through many ages (the historicist Protestant view)
A single future personal Antichrist, revealed after the apostasy in the last days
The Roman imperial line with its rage for deification (the emperor-cult)

themes: except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition
let no man deceive you by any means
the falling away and the man of sin
the crux of the man of sin's identity

schools.summary: The character of the man of sin: he 'opposeth (sets himself against, is the adversary) and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped (every object of worship)' — claiming a supremacy belonging to God alone; 'so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God' — the crowning impiety of arrogating divine honours. On 'the temple of God,' the grammatical-historical note both readings — figuratively the Christian church, or a literal temple — without forcing one (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin, Gill, and Henry take 'the temple of God' figuratively as the Christian church: the man of sin (the Papacy) usurps divine authority within the church, claiming honours due to God alone, the bishops of Rome having exalted themselves above God and earthly governors and received blasphemous titles ('Our Lord God the pope'). The seat 'in the temple of God' is thus the usurpation of God's authority in the church, not a literal building.

Cyril and the fathers take 'the temple of God' literally — the personal Antichrist will sit in a rebuilt temple at Jerusalem (linked to the abomination of desolation, Daniel; Matthew 24:15), shewing himself to be God in the last days.

debates.question: What is 'the temple of God' in which the man of sin sits?

sides.position: Figuratively the Christian church — the Papacy usurping divine authority within it
A literal rebuilt temple at Jerusalem, where the personal Antichrist sits

themes: who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped
so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God
the man of sin's blasphemous self-exaltation
the crux of the temple of God

schools.summary: Paul recalls his earlier oral teaching: 'remember ye not, that, when I was yet with you, I told you these things?' — he had already, during his ministry at Thessalonica, instructed them about the apostasy and the man of sin; this was no new doctrine, and the brevity and allusiveness of his written words here are explained by the fuller teaching they had already received orally; the design being that, forewarned, they should not be stumbled or shaken when these things came (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Remember ye not, that, when I was yet with you, I told you these things?
Paul's reminder of his prior teaching
the apostasy and man of sin no new doctrine
forewarned, not to be stumbled

schools.summary: The phrase 'what withholdeth (to katechon)' is neuter here, masculine in v.7 ('he that letteth, ho katechon') — a restraining principle or power, capable of being embodied in a person, then operating to hold back the man of sin's revealing 'that he might be revealed in his time (his appointed season),' only at the moment God has set. Barnes (with Chrysostom, Wesley) holds the restraint to be the civil power of Rome, whose removal (Constantine's transfer of the seat to Constantinople) would let the papal power rise; the early Christians so understood it and prayed for the empire's peace, knowing the man of sin would rise on its ruins.

Calvin differs from Chrysostom: the restrainer is not the Roman Empire but the necessary diffusion of the gospel through all the world — the universal call of the Gentiles must run its course, grace being offered to all, before God gives loose rein to Antichrist and ripens the world's malice for vengeance; this is the delay until the career of the gospel is completed. (Gill notes and rejects two further views: the apostle Paul himself, and the grace/gifts of the Spirit.)

debates.question: What is the restraining power ('what withholdeth')?

sides.position: The Roman Empire / civil power, whose removal lets the man of sin rise
The worldwide preaching of the gospel, which must run its course first
The grace/gifts of the Spirit, or the apostle Paul himself (noted and rejected)

themes: And now ye know what withholdeth that he might be revealed in his time
the restraining power that holds back the man of sin
the crux of the restrainer
revealed only in his appointed time

schools.summary: The evil was already secretly at work: 'the mystery of iniquity (mysterion tes anomias — the secret of lawlessness, the hidden working of the apostate principle) doth already work (energeitai — is operative)' — even in Paul's day the seeds of the coming apostasy were silently growing; the restraining power ('he who now letteth,' masculine ho katechon) will continue to restrain 'until he be taken out of the way (removed from the midst),' and the prophecy is satisfied if 'he that withholdeth' proves to be a whole succession of persons, not one man (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin and Henry: it is a 'mystery' because the wicked designs were concealed under fair shows and pretences of devotion; the corruption (Henry: of the Romish church) came in by degrees, secretly, the usurpation gradual not all at once — already begun in the apostles' day in the pride, ambition, and false teaching of men (the Nicolaitans, Diotrephes), shooting up in the sects that would choke God's field.

themes: For the mystery of iniquity doth already work
only he who now letteth will let, until he be taken out of the way
the mystery of iniquity already working
secret growth until the restrainer is removed

schools.summary: When the restraint is removed, 'then shall that Wicked (the lawless one, ho anomos) be revealed' — fully manifested in his open lawlessness, no longer secret but disclosed; 'whom the Lord shall consume (analoi — destroy, do away with) with the spirit (breath) of his mouth' — by the mere breath of his mouth (cf. Isaiah 11:4), the word and Spirit of Christ; 'and shall destroy (katargesei — bring to nought, abolish) with the brightness (epiphaneia — the manifestation, the bright shining) of his coming' — the splendour of Christ's appearing utterly abolishing the lawless one (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Cyril): the preaching of the gospel itself, the breath of Christ's mouth, begins even now to consume Antichrist's power, and the brightness of Christ's coming will at last abolish him entirely — a comfort to believers that the reign of the man of sin is temporary, its limits set by God.

themes: and then shall that Wicked be revealed
whom the Lord shall consume with the spirit of his mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of his coming
the revealing and destruction of the Wicked one
consumed by Christ's word and coming

schools.summary: The dark parody: 'whose coming (parousia — a coming that mimics Christ's own) is after (according to) the working (energeian — the energy, operation) of Satan' — the man of sin's manifestation empowered by Satan, an infernal counterfeit of the true Christ's coming; 'with all power and signs and lying wonders (terasin pseudous — wonders of falsehood, miracles that serve and confirm a lie)' — apparent miracles, whether real wonders wrought by Satan or deceptive shows, all in the service of falsehood (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the wonders are 'lying' both in their source and their end — they deceive, leading men away from the truth; the Antichrist works signs to seduce, that even the elect, were it possible, might be deceived.

themes: even him, whose coming is after the working of Satan
with all power and signs and lying wonders
the man of sin's coming with satanic power
lying wonders that serve a lie

schools.summary: The means and the objects: 'all deceivableness (every deceit, the whole art of deception) of unrighteousness in them that perish (the perishing)' — the man of sin's lies have full effect only on those who are perishing, the deception working on hearts already turned from truth; 'because they received not the love of the truth' — not merely the truth, but the love of it; they had no heart for the truth, did not welcome and cherish it, and so were left open to the lie; 'that they might be saved' — the love of the truth being the very means of salvation they refused, their ruin self-incurred (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: and with all deceivableness of unrighteousness in them that perish
because they received not the love of the truth, that they might be saved
the deception of those who perish
ruin for refusing the love of the truth

schools.summary: The solemn statement: 'for this cause (because they refused the love of the truth) God shall send (pempei — sends) them strong delusion (energeian planes — a working of error, a powerful deceiving influence)' — God himself, in righteous judgment, gives them over to be deceived; not creating the lie or tempting to evil, but, in just retribution for their willful rejection of truth, withdrawing his restraining grace and giving them up to the delusion they chose — a judicial hardening (cf. Romans 1:24-28), permitting and ordering the deception as punishment (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the delusion is sent 'that they should believe a lie (the falsehood)' — having rejected the truth, they are justly left to embrace the lie; the punishment fits the sin, error being the righteous recompense for the despisers of truth, God ordering even their deception to his just ends.

themes: And for this cause God shall send them strong delusion, that they should believe a lie
God's judicial sending of strong delusion
judicial hardening, not authoring sin
error the recompense for despising truth

schools.summary: The purpose of the judicial delusion: 'that they all might be damned (krithosin — judged, condemned) who believed not the truth' — the end of God's giving them over is their just condemnation, judged for refusing to believe the truth set before them, their unbelief willful not innocent ignorance; 'but had pleasure (eudokesantes — took delight, found satisfaction) in unrighteousness' — the reason they refused the truth was that they loved sin, the will (not the intellect) the seat of their ruin, preferring iniquity and so rejecting the truth that condemned it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: they perish not from ignorance but from love of sin; having taken pleasure in unrighteousness, they would not receive the truth, and so are justly condemned — the moral root of unbelief laid bare.

themes: That they all might be damned who believed not the truth, but had pleasure in unrighteousness
the condemnation of those who loved unrighteousness
willful unbelief judged
pleasure in sin the root of unbelief

schools.summary: The turn from the perishing to the saved: 'but we are bound (owe it as a debt) to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord'; 'because God hath from the beginning (ap' arches — from eternity, or from the first) chosen (heilato — selected) you to salvation' — their salvation resting on God's choice; 'through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth' — the election unto salvation by means of the Spirit's setting-apart and renewing work and the believing of the truth, the end salvation and the means the Spirit and faith (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Gill): God's choosing 'from the beginning' is gracious, unconditional, eternal election to salvation — the security of the saved set in sharp contrast with the perishing of v.12; salvation flows from God's free choice, accomplished through the Spirit's sanctification and faith, not from foreseen merit.

themes: God hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation
through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth
thanksgiving for their election to salvation
chosen through the Spirit and faith

schools.summary: Election issues in calling: 'whereunto (to which salvation) he called you by our gospel' — God's eternal choice is carried out in time through the call of the gospel, Paul's preaching being the means by which God summoned them to the salvation he had chosen; 'by our gospel' the appointed instrument of the effectual call, election and calling joined (the one the eternal purpose, the other its execution); 'to the obtaining (peripoiesin — acquiring, gaining possession) of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the end being that believers should share in Christ's own glory (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the calling is unto a participation in the glory of the Lord Jesus Christ — believers are summoned by the gospel not merely to escape judgment but to obtain and share his glory, the high end of their election.

themes: Whereunto he called you by our gospel
to the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ
the calling by the gospel
the goal of sharing Christ's glory

schools.summary: Paul's practical conclusion ('therefore,' from their election and calling): 'stand fast (steko — stand firm, hold your ground)' — being chosen and called, they are to be steadfast, not shaken by the false alarms or errors that troubled them; 'and hold (krateite — hold firmly, grasp, retain) the traditions (paradoseis — the teachings handed down, the body of apostolic instruction) which ye have been taught, whether by word (Paul's oral teaching when present) or our epistle (his letters)' — clinging to the apostolic doctrine itself, delivered both by speech and by writing, equally to be held (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Therefore, brethren, stand fast, and hold the traditions which ye have been taught
whether by word, or our epistle
the charge to stand fast and hold the apostolic traditions
the apostolic teaching by word and letter

schools.summary: Paul prays to both: 'now our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father' — Christ named first, then the Father, with (in the Greek) a singular verb following; the joint address with a singular verb a witness to the unity and equal dignity of the Son with the Father; God 'hath loved us, and hath given us everlasting consolation (aionian paraklesin — eternal comfort, comfort that does not fail) and good hope through grace' — the believer's comfort everlasting, resting on God's unchanging love, and the hope a well-grounded hope of glory given not by merit but freely through grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: now our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God, even our Father, which hath loved us
hath given us everlasting consolation and good hope through grace
the prayer to Christ and the Father
everlasting consolation and good hope by grace

schools.summary: The content of Paul's prayer (the singular verb governing both Christ and the Father, v.16): 'comfort (parakalesai — console, encourage) your hearts, and stablish (sterixai — make firm, strengthen, confirm) you' — that God would both console them inwardly and steady them firmly; the first petition answering their troubled state (2:2), calming and comforting the hearts the false alarms and persecutions had shaken; 'in every good word and work' — establishing them in all right doctrine ('word') and right conduct ('work'), the establishing issuing in steadfast truth and holy living (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Comfort your hearts, and stablish you in every good word and work
the prayer that God comfort and stablish them
consoling the troubled and confirming them
established in right doctrine and conduct
